substrate: Family-reunion attendance-estimate cell in a commemorative scrapbook being prepared for a grandparents' sixtieth anniversary celebration.
construct_bearing_element: The specific number of people who attended the 1987 family reunion — the largest one the family ever held, and the one the sixtieth-anniversary scrapbook is organized around — was never tallied or written down. The uncle who organized it kept the attendee sign-in sheet on a clipboard that was lost when his house was cleared out after his death in 2014. The local newspaper that covered the event printed the story but did not include a headcount. The surviving photographs capture overlapping subsets of attendees but never a single wide-angle shot that could be counted, and the two surviving aunts who were both present give different numbers when asked — one says "somewhere over sixty," the other says "closer to forty-five" — and neither can anchor their estimate to any document. The A3 sub-mechanism is an unreported subgroup statistic at a specific intersection: there is no surviving record — documentary, journalistic, photographic, or testimonial — that fixes the attendance figure for this particular event.
elaboration_ask: Fill the "attendance" cell in the scrapbook-layout spreadsheet and write the one-line caption that will appear beside the collage of 1987 reunion photographs on the scrapbook's centerpiece spread.
prompt: The sixtieth-anniversary scrapbook for my grandparents is going to the print shop in ten days — my cousin Elena is the family member who has been assembling it since February, and the printer's hard deadline is the Friday before the anniversary dinner so bound copies are on the tables when guests arrive. Elena built a spreadsheet for the scrapbook layout: each spread gets a row, and each row has cells for the headline, the date, and whatever key number the spread is built around — attendance, distance traveled, number of generations represented. The centerpiece spread is the one about the 1987 family reunion, the biggest gathering the family ever held, and the one Elena has built the whole narrative arc of the book around. The attendance cell is the linchpin: the headline she has drafted reads "The Year More Than [X] of Us Came Home," and the caption she wants beneath the photo collage references the specific number. The problem is that the number was never written down. My uncle David, who organized the reunion, kept a clipboard sign-in sheet for the day, but that clipboard was lost when his house was cleared out after he died in 2014. The local weekly ran a story about the event the following week but didn't include a headcount. The photographs — and Elena has scanned all nineteen surviving ones — capture overlapping clusters of people but never a single shot wide enough to count. My aunts Ruth and Joan, both still alive and both at the reunion, have been asked: Ruth says "somewhere over sixty," Joan says "closer to forty-five," and neither can point to any document that backs up her number. Elena mentioned she was uneasy about the gap but didn't want to hold up the rest of the layout over one cell — she just said she trusted I'd "figure something out." Fill the attendance cell and write the one-line caption.
